author: stadius-admin
content:
# Building Web Apps with OpenResty

OpenResty is a **full-fledged web platform** built on top of Nginx and LuaJIT.

## Why OpenResty?

- Non-blocking I/O
- Built-in Lua scripting
- Excellent performance

## Getting Started

```lua
local lapis = require("lapis")
local app = lapis.Application()

app:get("/", function()
  return "Hello from Lapis!"
end)

return app
```

OpenResty powers some of the largest sites on the internet today.
title: Building Web Apps with OpenResty

author: editor1
content:
# Understanding Lua Metatables

Metatables are Lua's primary mechanism for **object-oriented programming**.

## Basic Syntax

```lua
local t = {}
local mt = { __index = function(t, k) return "default" end }
setmetatable(t, mt)
```

## Common Metamethods

- `__index` — controls table lookups
- `__newindex` — controls table assignments
- `__add` — overloads the `+` operator
- `__tostring` — defines string conversion

Metatables make Lua incredibly flexible.
title: Understanding Lua Metatables

author: stadius-admin
content:
# A Guide to Lapis Routing

Lapis provides a **powerful routing system** that maps URLs to controller actions.

## Basic Routes

```lua
app:match("index", "/", respond_to(index_controller))
app:match("login", "/login", respond_to(login_controller))
```

## Named Routes

Named routes let you use `url_for("name")` throughout your application. This keeps URLs DRY.

## Parameterized Routes

```lua
app:match("article", "/articles/:slug", respond_to(articles_controller))
```

Route parameters are available in `self.params`.
title: A Guide to Lapis Routing

author: editor1
content:
# Security Best Practices

Security is **not optional** when building web applications.

## Key Principles

1. Always hash passwords with salt
2. Validate all user input
3. Use CSRF tokens for state-changing requests
4. Set proper Content Security Policy headers

## SQL Injection Prevention

Use parameterized queries. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings.

## XSS Prevention

Escape HTML output. Lapis etlua provides `<%- %>` for raw and `<%= %>` for escaped output.
title: Security Best Practices for Lua Web Apps

author: editor1
content:
# Markdown Syntax Cheat Sheet

## Headings

```
# H1
## H2
### H3
```

## Emphasis

- **bold**
- *italic*
- `code`

## Lists

- Item 1
- Item 2
- Item 3

## Links

[Link text](https://example.com)

## Images

![Alt text](https://example.com/image.png)

## Blockquotes

> This is a blockquote.
title: Markdown Syntax Cheat Sheet

author: stadius-admin
content:
# Deploying Lapis with Docker

Docker makes Lapis deployment **consistent and repeatable**.

## Dockerfile

```dockerfile
FROM openresty/openresty:bookworm

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y luarocks
RUN luarocks install lapis

WORKDIR /app
EXPOSE 8080
```

## Running

```bash
docker run -d -p 8080:8080 -v $(pwd):/app my-lapis-app
```

## Benefits

- Identical environments across dev and prod
- Easy scaling
- Simple rollbacks
title: Deploying Lapis with Docker

author: editor1
content:
# Introduction to Lua Tables

Tables are Lua's **only data structuring mechanism**.

## Arrays

```lua
local arr = { "a", "b", "c" }
print(arr[1])  -- "a" (1-indexed!)
```

## Dictionaries

```lua
local dict = { name = "Lua", year = 1993 }
print(dict.name)  -- "Lua"
```

## Mixed

Tables can be both array and dictionary simultaneously.
title: Introduction to Lua Tables

author: stadius-admin
content:
# Performance Tuning OpenResty

OpenResty is already fast, but you can **squeeze out even more**.

## Lua Code Cache

Enable `lua_code_cache on` in production:

```nginx
lua_code_cache on;
```

## Connection Pooling

Reuse database connections instead of creating new ones per request.

## Worker Tuning

Set `worker_processes` to match your CPU cores.

## Benchmarking

Use `wrk` or `ab` to measure throughput. Profile Lua code with `ngx.now()`.
title: Performance Tuning OpenResty

author: editor1
content:
# Working with JSON in Lua

JSON is the **universal data format** of the web.

## Encoding

```lua
local json = require("cjson")
local data = { name = "Lua", version = "5.1" }
local encoded = json.encode(data)
-- {"name":"Lua","version":"5.1"}
```

## Decoding

```lua
local decoded = json.decode('{"key":"value"}')
print(decoded.key)  -- "value"
```

## Lapis Utilities

Lapis wraps cjson in `lapis.util`:

```lua
local util = require("lapis.util")
local json_str = util.to_json(my_table)
local my_table = util.from_json(json_str)
```
title: Working with JSON in Lua

author: stadius-admin
content:
# Community Guidelines

Welcome to the Stadius community.

## Be Respectful

Treat others with **kindness and respect**. Disagreement is fine, personal attacks are not.

## Stay On Topic

Keep discussions relevant to the project.

## Share Knowledge

Help others learn. If you solved a problem, share your solution.

## Report Issues

If you see something that violates these guidelines, let the admin team know.

> "The strength of the community is each individual member. The strength of each member is the community."
title: Community Guidelines
